findings.issue: Auto plan-size detection via cgroup + procfs is net-new, brittle infrastructure code with no precedent in the project, and it is the dominant complexity driver of the feature while being explicitly only a heuristic ('не гарантия точного момента OOM'). The spec already provides --backend-killer.plan-size (explicit override) and --backend-killer.size-multiplier as controls.
findings.why_matters: noisia has zero existing procfs/cgroup parsing code; all workloads are thin pgx loops. cgroup v2 memory.max can be the literal string 'max', v1 paths differ across kernels, and inside a container /proc/meminfo reports host RAM (the spec itself acknowledges all three pitfalls). The heuristic also only works when noisia is co-located with PG — for remote PG it is meaningless and the user must override anyway. This adds the feature's biggest test/maintenance surface and brittleness to deliver a convenience that is fundamentally approximate and frequently inapplicable.
findings.fix: Consider deferring auto-detection for the first cut: ship a sane default plan-size + size-multiplier + explicit override, and let the DBA tune on the stand (their job in the demo). If auto-detection is kept, scope it tightly to cgroup v2 only with a clean fallback to a default constant (not full procfs+cgroupv1 parsing), and make the brittleness/co-location requirement an explicit acceptance criterion rather than a heuristic buried in edge cases.

findings.issue: The spec describes the leak mechanism as 'continuously issue unique PREPARE' but does not pin down HOW it is issued through the db.Conn interface relative to pgx v5's own statement-handling. pgx v5 with the extended protocol maintains its own client-side statement cache and prepares statements implicitly.
findings.why_matters: If the workload calls conn.Exec(ctx, 'PREPARE stmtN AS SELECT $1::int') as literal SQL text it correctly leaks SERVER-side plan cache (the intended effect). But if implemented as pgx-level prepared statements, the leak semantics and where memory grows differ, and unique names matter differently. The code-research notes the literal-SQL approach ('SELECT $1::int' with a unique name) which is the right one — but the user-spec is the WHAT/WHY artifact and the mechanism is feasible, so this is a low-risk note to verify at tech-spec time, not a blocker.
findings.fix: No change needed in user-spec (mechanism detail belongs in tech-spec). Flag for tech-spec: confirm the leak is driven by literal 'PREPARE <unique-name> AS ...' SQL via conn.Exec so the growth is server-side backend plan cache, and validate that target-list width (plan-size) actually scales per-statement memory as assumed.

findings.issue: The 'culmination' detection relies on distinguishing an OOM-induced connection drop from any other connection error while ctx is still live. The spec says to print the culmination line 'при обрыве собственного соединения (OOM) и активном context', but a transient network error or admin-issued pg_terminate_backend would produce the same broken-connection error and trigger a false 'target likely OOM-restarted' message.
findings.why_matters: In a teaching context a misleading culmination line ('target likely OOM-restarted') asserted when the instance did NOT actually OOM undermines the educational value. The spec already hedges with 'likely', which is reasonable, but the behavior on a non-OOM mid-run error is not addressed.
findings.fix: Acceptance criteria already say any conn error with live ctx prints culmination + returns nil. Confirm this is intended even for non-OOM disconnects, or add a note that the message is best-effort ('likely') and that a non-OOM disconnect is treated identically by design. No structural change required.

summary: Approved: the core mechanism (single db.Connect session leaking unique server-side PREPAREs to grow backend RSS until OOM, with an in-process self-report panel and optional pg_backend_memory_contexts read) is fully feasible on the existing pgx v5 / db-interface / kingpin / rate stack, correctly single-threaded, and well right-sized at M with the testcontainers mechanics-only test approach. Main concern is the auto plan-size detection via cgroup+procfs — net-new brittle infrastructure that is the feature's largest complexity/maintenance surface yet only a co-location-dependent heuristic already backed by explicit override and multiplier; consider deferring or tightly scoping it for the first cut.
